list.name: Eothraim
list.description: Superb equestrian warriors, semi-nomadic herders, and hunters who move between seasonal pastures and maintain a disunified but highly honorable society.

list.name: Gramuz
list.description: Brave, resilient foresters and horse-herders with strong warrior traditions.

list.name: Sagath
list.description: Nomadic horsemen and chariot-riders who frequent the pastures.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Woodmen
ONLY ADD this region id in entity Beornings

list.name: Northmen
list.description: Descendants of Vidugavia’s horse-lords in the plains and East Bight, formerly prosperous farmers and horse-breeders, reduced by the Plague to hard-edged defenders of isolated homesteads and fortified camps.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Eothraim

list.name: Gondorian settlers
list.description: Gondorians of Calenardhron and Dor Rhûnen who garrison forts like Thorontir and farm pockets such as Tharlorien and the Undeeps, guarding the fords and asserting Gondor’s claimed dominion.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Sagath
ONLY ADD region id in entity Silvan Elves

list.name: Radagast the Brown
list.description: The Wizard dwelling at Rhosgobel on the western edge of Mirkwood, devoting his power and attention to birds and beasts rather than politics, and providing a rare point of light and refuge in the shadowed woods.

list.name: The Necromancer of Dol Guldur
list.description: Sauron in his guise as the Necromancer, occupying Dol Guldur since the 12th century T.A., radiating fear and corruption, breeding Orcs, trolls, spiders and fell beasts, and acting as the dark center of Southern Mirkwood.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD this region id in entity common orcs

list.name: Thranduil’s Silvan elves
list.description: Silvan Elves under Thranduil living in caverns and halls near the eastern eaves of the forest, lovers of feasting, song and languages and largely indifferent to neighboring Men and Dwarves unless threatened.

list.name: Dwarves of the Iron Hills
list.description: Durin’s Folk who mine and smelt rich iron deposits in the Iron Hills east of Mirkwood, providing metalwork, weapons and armor and maintaining trade links with Dalemen and Lake-men.

list.name: Dwarves of Erebor
list.description: The Dwarves of the Lonely Mountain, dwelling in a great isolated peak north of Long Lake and using its caverns as halls, mines and treasure vaults; tightly connected to trade along the River Running.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Northmen
ONLY ADD region id in entity Beornings
ONLY ADD region id in entity common orcs
ONLY ADD region id in entity Rohirrim
ONLY ADD region id in entity Variags

list.name: Asdriags
list.description: A savage, wain-riding Easterling people who wander the grasslands in search of plunder.

list.name: Wandering Eothraim
list.description: Semi-nomadic herders and horse-masters who clash with the Easterling tribes over pasture lands.

list.name: Dúnedain and Gondorian farmers of Anórien
list.description: The founding of Gondor concentrated people in the Anduin valley, settling Anórien, Ithilien and Lebennin together as early heartlands.[file:216] By the War of the Ring, Anórien’s lowlands host scattered villages and farmsteads of Gondorians of mixed Númenórean and local descent, who work fields and pastures and supply traffic along the Great West Road and river.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Woses

list.name: Messengers, soldiers and Rohirrim riders
list.description: Anórien’s Great West Road is heavily used by Gondorian messengers and troops, and during the War of the Ring the Rohirrim ride through its northern strip on their way to Minas Tirith; Tolkien explicitly describes the beacon fires behind Anórien and the riders answering them.[web:234][web:235] The region’s population therefore includes transient military columns and supply trains on top of the resident peasantry.

list.name: Marshmen
list.description: Small, long-lived descendants of the Edain dwelling in Nindalf and western marshes, living by hunting, fishing, goat herding and gardening, worshipping Eru (Iroi) and the Valar as Jevinn and Eremi, and organizing themselves through yearly councils on the holy island of Tel Belarin; some have been driven westward by the Dead Marshes’ corruption.[file:37]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Gondorian settlers
ONLY ADD region id in entity Asdriags

list.name: Urban Northmen
list.description: Merchants and settlers from the Nan Celduin valley, speaking Rhovanion and Westron, who came to share in eastern trade and now inhabit fortified towns, culturally close to Gondor but proud of their Northman identity.[file:37]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity common orcs
ONLY ADD region id in entity Orcs of Khazad-dum

list.name: Bandits and deserters
list.description: Disaffected soldiers, plague-maddened peasants and local rogues haunting the roads and ruins, turning the depopulated frontier into a patchwork of robber bands unconcerned with Gondor or Sauron beyond what they can steal.[file:37]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Woses

list.name: Gondorian Quarrymen
list.description: Gondorian laborers who occasionally work the ancient limestone quarries (like Nardol Quarry) on the forest's northern margins.

list.name: Dwarves of the Iron Hills
list.description: Durin’s Folk who mine and smelt rich iron deposits in the Iron Hills east of Mirkwood, providing metalwork, weapons and armor and maintaining trade links with Dalemen and Lake-men.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dwarves of Erebor
ONLY ADD region id in entity Northmen
ONLY ADD region id in entity Beornings
ONLY ADD region id in entity common orcs

list.name: Dúnedain and Sea-lords of Lebennin
list.description: High Men of Gondor, especially the Sea-lords whose power is rooted in Pelargir and the coastal towns; they command fleets, administer the province and lead the struggle against Corsairs and Haradan privateers.[file:30]

list.name: Urban folk of Pelargir and river towns
list.description: Merchants, artisans, sailors and laborers crowd Pelargir—the largest and oldest city of Gondor—and the lesser ports, living from shipbuilding, trade and supplying the Royal Fleet and merchant traffic.[file:30]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity dunlendings
ONLY ADD region id in entity woses

list.name: Marshmen of the Ethir Anduin
list.description: Hardy folk of the Anduin delta and coastal marshes who know the shifting channels and shallows; some act as guides or smugglers in the Ethir Anduin labyrinth.[file:30]

list.name: Smugglers
list.description: Semi-legal privateers, outright pirates and smugglers operate from coves, swamps and hidden harbors along the coasts and the Ethir Anduin, raiding trade and dodging royal duties despite the Sea-lords’ efforts.[file:30]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Corsairs

list.name: Eraguk
list.description: Fierce steppe nomads of mixed Haruze and Nórniag lineage, the Eraguk roamed the Aegardh since ancient times as the original inhabitants; after centuries of Gondorian pressure, most were driven east or assimilated, surviving in small, often bandit-style groups by T.A. 1640.[file:73]

list.name: Haruze
list.description: Haruze farmers and nomads who have occupied the Vale of Harnen, growing sorghum and herding flocks, moving seasonally between Harad/Nurn and the southern Harondor plateau; a persistent cultural presence even when Gondor claims political control.[file:73]

list.name: Gondorian rangers
list.description: Gondor maintains border fortresses like Amon Eithel and patrols the Hyarmenti road to keep the Aegardh nominally under control; these rangers are thin on the ground and frequently harassed by nomads, Haruze and Umbarean agents.[file:73]

list.name: Umbarean agents
list.description: After the Kin-strife, Umbarean and Haruze captains encouraged nomadic clans to migrate into the Aegardh, using Harondor as a staging ground against Gondor and the Hyarmenti trade road.[file:73]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain

list.name: Daen folk
list.description: The indigenous pre-Númenórean people of the coastlands and plains, shorter and stockier than the Dúnedain, who adopted Westron and much of Númenórean culture while retaining their oral traditions and ballads; they form the majority of the common population of Lebennin and Anfalas.[file:73]

list.name: Daen Coentis
list.description: Mountain clans of the White Mountains living above the timberline in primitive groupings; semi-nomadic goat shepherds and fisherfolk on the coasts (Dunir of Andrast), they have largely avoided cultural contact with Gondor and still honor ancient Daen traditions.[file:73]

list.name: Sakalai
list.description: Small, dark-haired, independent tribes in the Loeg Balimur and remote Ethir Anduin, believed to be of mixed Daen and Southron stock; they live as swamp fishermen largely outside Gondorian authority.[file:73]

list.name: Grey-elves of Edhellond
list.description: A remnant Sindarin community dwelling at the fabled haven of white swan-ships near the mouth of the Morthond, closely allied with the Princes of Belfalas; guardians of the Eryn Laegol and builders of the most beautiful ships in Southern Gondor.[file:73]

ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain

list.name: Bree-landers
list.description: Mixed folk of Bree and its environs, including men of various stocks and hobbits, living in and around a trenched, hedged village at the junction of the Greenway and Great East Road, once a vibrant trade-town but fading by T.A. 1640.[file:25]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain
ONLY ADD region id in entity dunlendings

list.name: Tharbad people
list.description: Merchants, craftsmen, guildsmen, smugglers and refugees crowd the fen‑ringed city of Tharbad, whose power shifts from Cardolan’s kings to Gondor’s Canotar and then to powerful guilds as war, flood and plague erode royal authority.[file:31]

list.name: Saralainn
list.description: Rowdy, semi‑independent clans of Saralainn and the Gwathló mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain
ONLY ADD region id in entity dunlendings

list.name: Beffraen
list.description: A relatively primitive folk akin to Drúedain, inhabiting the dark woods of the Rast Vorn peninsula; they are secretive, use the treacherous forest and coasts to remain largely outside Dúnadan control and maintain their own customs.[file:31]

list.name: Saralainn
list.description: Rowdy, semi‑independent clans of Saralainn and the Gwathló mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]

list.name: Rangers of Ithilien
list.description: Gondorian rangers who launch stealth raids against Sauron's forces.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain
ONLY ADD region id in entity Daen folk
ONLY ADD region id in entity Daen Coentis
ONLY ADD region id in entity Sakalai
ONLY ADD region id in entity Grey-elves of Edhellond

list.name: Princes of Dol Amroth
list.description: Hereditary Dúnedain rulers of Dor‑en‑Ernil, holding a status just below the King and Steward; their line carries Elven blood and rules almost autonomously from Dol Amroth, symbolized by a silver Swan‑ship on deep blue.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain

list.name: Eredrim
list.description: Dunlending‑related highlanders of the Ered Tarthonion, descendants of the Daen Coentis; rugged village dwellers ruled by their own chieftains, paying tribute in kind, practicing animistic and sometimes dark rites and resisting assimilation.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dunlendings

list.name: Elves of Edhellond
list.description: Sindar and Silvan Elves of the Elf‑haven in the Morthond delta; Sindar provide shipwrights, captains, and leaders, while Silvan Elves form most of the common folk, hunters, and fishers.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Corsairs

people_field_by_region.description: Dor‑en‑Ernil’s population mixes Dúnedain princes and lowland commoners with Eredrim highlanders descended from the Daen Coentis, independent Elves of Edhellond, and sailors, merchants, privateers, smugglers, and Corsair enemies across the bay.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Dunlendings

list.name: Daen Iontis
list.description: Militant Dunnish faction exemplified by clan Arailt and their allies, bitterly hating Dúnedain, closely linked to the Temple of Justice, and pushing for war and a harsh, sacrificial religion that twists old Daen beliefs.[file:32]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Daen Coentis
ONLY ADD region id in entity Dúnedain

list.name: Stoors of New Maresh
list.description: A small Stoor Hobbit community living in burrows on hills rising from a marsh near the Dunstrem, traveling by pole‑boats, trading herbs and fish, and protected by the treacherous wetlands and their own wariness of outsiders.[file:32]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Woses

list.name: Merchants
list.description: Merchants based in Larach Duhnnan and other sites move goods between clans and to Tharbad, while a hidden Smugglers’ Hold grows gort and tartella herbs for Corsairs of Umbar using Dunnish and Dúnadan frontmen.[file:32]

people_field_by_region.military: Each Chief keeps from 100-400 Warriors as a permanent retinue. Each Clan can also raise 1,400-3,200 Warriors given 1-4 weeks. Tribes favor light armor and carry spears and melee weapons predominately.
people_field_by_region.description: Dunland is dominated by Dunlendings—Daen Lintis highlanders descended from the Daen Coentis—living in extended family bands within clans, but also includes Dúnedain estates, Stoors, Drúedain in Caerdh Wood, organized traders, bards, healers and a powerful Dark‑influenced Temple hierarchy.

list.name: Falathrim
list.description: Descendants of the ancient mariners of the Falas, the Falathrim are the primary coastal Elves of Lindon, inhabiting Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond and maintaining shipbuilding and ocean-voyage traditions. They form the backbone of Lindon’s maritime culture and operate the Grey Havens as the main port to Valinor.[file:99]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Noldor

list.name: Iathrim and Laegrim
list.description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindon’s woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

list.name: Lindellim
list.description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and Rhûnlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of Lhûn; Thorin’s Halls (Durin’s Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Hobbits

people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
people_field_by_region.description: Lindon’s population is overwhelmingly Elvish, composed of multiple lineages (Noldor, Sindar, Laiquendi, Nandor, Falathrim) under Gil-galad and later Círdan, with a significant Dwarven presence in the Blue Mountains and peripheral contact with Men and Hobbits via routes and harbors. The Traveller’s Guide provides detailed population tables showing long-term decline through the Third Age.

people_field_by_region.description: Lebennin’s population is a layered mix: Númenórean-descended Dúnedain and urban Gondorians in the cities and ports, rural folk and Dunnish highlanders in the uplands, Drúedain in remote mountain valleys, Marshmen in the Ethir Anduin, and a constant presence of Sea-lords, sailors and smugglers bound up with the Bay of Belfalas.

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Beornings

list.name: Dwarves of Durin
list.description: Durin’s heirs and their people who, after the loss of Moria, established mining colonies at Silverplunge, Thunderclift, Silverpit, Long Peak and Norr-dum, and later reconnected with Erebor; ruled by a king advised by Dwarf-lords and by appointed Wardens in outlying colonies.[file:38]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Beornings

list.name: Ice-orcs
list.description: Tall, gaunt, pale, sun-tolerant Orcs descended from Morgoth’s northern guards, ruling the Forodwaith from the Dark-Ice Fortress and mining outposts like Thollakar; they raid neighboring Lotan and Lossoth, worship Morgoth in gruesome festivals, and now answer uneasily to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:38]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity common orcs

list.name: Beornings / Beijabar (T.A. 1640)
list.description: Tall, heavy-boned, fur-clad Northmen practicing the cult of the Bear and shape-changing, living in scattered family steads in the Narrows and highlands near the mountains; governed informally by household elders and spiritually led by a High Shape-changer (Waetan) who can call the Faird Levy in times of crisis.[file:38]

list.name: Dwarves of Durinâ€™s folk
list.description: Durinâ€™s heirs and their people who, after the loss of Moria, established mining colonies at Silverplunge, Thunderclift, Silverpit, Long Peak and Norr-dum, and later reconnected with Erebor; ruled by a king advised by Dwarf-lords and by appointed Wardens in outlying colonies.[file:38]

list.name: Beornings (later period c. 2580)
list.description: By the time Dragons press hardest, Beornings in the Narrows are a people under siege, losing numbers to wolves, Orcs, Angmarim and Dragons, with many migrating south to their kin in the Anduin vales as settlements fail.[file:38]

list.name: Ice-orcs of Kala Dulakurth
list.description: Tall, gaunt, pale, sun-tolerant Orcs descended from Morgothâ€™s northern guards, ruling the Forodwaith from the Dark-Ice Fortress and mining outposts like Thollakar; they raid neighboring Lotan and Lossoth, worship Morgoth in gruesome festivals, and now answer uneasily to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:38]

list.name: Orc tribes of the Grey Mountains
list.description: Three main tribesâ€”Lor-uruk-shab, Uruk-erag and Asharagâ€”hold strategic passes and foothills, nominally independent but increasingly forced to send tribute and soldiers to Mount Gundabad under Angmarâ€™s pressure; each ruled absolutely by a warlord.[file:38]

list.name: Lossoth and northern Mannish tribes
list.description: Human groups in the Forodwaith and tundra who hunt Losrandir and Caru and are frequent targets of Ice-orc raids and dragon harassment, especially from nomad drake Klyaxar and others roaming the Northern Waste.[file:38]

people_field_by_region.military: 150 Professional Warriors, many mounted. 150-200 drilled militia troops. 300-500 raw levy, used only in emergencies and takes 2-5 weeks to raise.
people_field_by_region.description: The Grey Mountains region is sparsely populated but contested by Beornings in the Narrows, Dwarves of Durinâ€™s folk in mining colonies, Ice-orcs in northern fortresses and outposts, and several Orc tribes in passes and foothills, with Dragons, Undead and agents of Angmar and Gundabad influencing politics and warfare.[file:38]

list.name: Elves of Ronalindon
list.description: Green-elves and sea-elves living in scattered coastal hamlets, engaged in fishing, woodcarving, and song.

people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
people_field_by_region.description: A quiet, sparse population of Laiquendi and Falathrim Elves who lead simple, maritime-focused lives.

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Descendants of the Firebeards and Broadbeams who maintained Belegost and Nogrod. They are skilled miners, smiths, and weapon-crafters, mining abundant copper, iron, and tin.

list.name: Mountain Giants
list.description: Extremely long-lived, reclusive beings of immense size who dwell in the high peaks and avoid contact with other races.

people_field_by_region.symbol: A Hammer surmounted by Three Tongues of Flame
people_field_by_region.military: 490 Warriors. 2,100 Warrior Levy.
people_field_by_region.description: The Ered Luin is primarily inhabited by the Dwarves of Nogrod and Belegost's descendants, alongside scattered Elven wanderers and mountain Giants.

list.name: Corsairs of Umbar

The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and RhÃ»nlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of LhÃ»n; Thorinâ€™s Halls (Durinâ€™s Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

list.name: Hobbits and Men along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ route
list.description: Hobbits of the Shire and Men of Cardolan do not form part of Lindonâ€™s resident population, but the Travellerâ€™s Guide describes their lands along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ 600-mile route from Imladris to Mithlond via Eregion, the South Downs, Barrow-downs, Old Forest and Brandywine. Tooks in Harlindon and Shire traditions about departures from the Grey Havens illustrate ongoing contacts.[file:99]

people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).

list.name: Sindar and Laiquendi of Harlindon
list.description: Forest-dwelling Elves who maintain quiet woodland villages, traditional rituals, and woodcraft.

list.name: Dwarf Miners of Harlindon
list.description: Dwarves operating coal and iron mines on the eastern slopes of the southern Blue Mountains.

people_field_by_region.symbol: CÃ­rdan's Device: A White Pelannun on a Blue Field
people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
people_field_by_region.description: Inhabitants are primarily Sindar and Laiquendi Elves who prefer a woodland life, along with some Dwarven coal miners in the east.

list.name: Falathrim (Sea-Elves)
list.description: Descendants of the ancient mariners of the Falas, the Falathrim are the primary coastal Elves of Lindon, inhabiting Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond and maintaining shipbuilding and ocean-voyage traditions. They form the backbone of Lindonâ€™s maritime culture and operate the Grey Havens as the main port to Valinor.[file:99]

list.name: Godhellim (Noldor of Lindon)
list.description: Noldorin Elves (Godhellim) under Gil-galad, primarily residing in Forlindon; they provide high leadership, craft and lore. Population tables show their proportion shrinking steadily from the Second to the late Third Age as many depart for Aman, while Sindar and Nandor proportions rise.[file:99]

list.name: Iathrim and Laegrim (Sindar and Laiquendi)
list.description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindonâ€™s woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Dwarven Vale
list.description: Firebeards and Broadbeams living in the fourteen holds of Nan-i-Naugrim. They maintain active smithing traditions and weapon-crafting at Merlost.

people_field_by_region.symbol: A Hammer surmounted by Three Tongues of Flame
people_field_by_region.military: 490 Warriors. 2,100 Warrior Levy.
people_field_by_region.description: The primary population of the vales is Dwarves of Durin's Folk and descendants of Belegost and Nogrod.

list.name: Nenedain (Northmen of the Anduin Vales)
list.description: Tall, strong, fair, hardy men descended from the same ancestral stock as the Edain; they form the majority of the settled population of the Anduin Vales, living as farmers, pastoralists and merchants. Culturally influenced by Gondor through trade, they have developed minor urban centers like Maethelburg along the Anduin but remain mostly a scattered rural folk. Decimated by the Great Plague (T.A. 1635â€“36), which killed about half their number. On good terms with DÃºnedain and tolerable terms with Hillmen; deeply hostile to Orcs.[file:88]

list.name: Beornings
list.description: An ancient and unique Northmen clan centered around the Holy Carrock on the eastern Anduin bank; the largest of all Northmen, as tall as DÃºnedain and broader. Vegetarians and legendary bakers (honey-cakes of Wilderland fame). Customarily shy and solitary but passionately jovial among kin; berserkers in battle. A rare few are hereditary shape-changers who can assume the form of great bears; the chieftain (Wealdanbera, 'Ruling Bear') is always a shape-changer, presiding over the secretive bear-cult (Beracynn).[file:88]

list.name: Hillmen of Rhudaur (NeDreubhan)
list.description: Short, dark, stocky men of the rugged hills of Rhudaur, distantly related to Dunlendings; superb mountaineers but deeply suspicious of outsiders, prone to treachery and ambush. Worship vengeful petty deities with blood sacrifice. Traditional enemies of both DÃºnedain and Northmen; greatly fear Elves. Some served in the hosts of the Witch-king of Angmar alongside Orcs, an uneasy alliance. Survive as hunters, gatherers and occasional brigands in the broken terrain.[file:88]

list.name: Orcs of Goblin-gate (Uruk-tarkhnarb)
list.description: The Orcs of the High Pass, led by the Great Goblin (Durba-hai, 'People-ruler'); an autonomous Orcish tribe paying nominal tribute to Angmar and Mount Gundabad. Composed of common Orcs (3â€“5 ft) and an elite of Uruk-hai (5â€“6 ft), who act as leaders and bodyguards. Expert miners, smiths and warriors; operate primarily at night (common Orcs are weakened by daylight; Uruk-hai are unaffected). The tribe holds the mountain passages by force and extorts or destroys all who cross without safe-conduct.[file:88]

list.name: Great Eagles of Eagles' Eyrie
list.description: The Great Eagles under their Lord hold a distinct political power in the regionâ€”not mere animals but intelligent beings of near-divine descent, capable of speech in Westron. They intervene selectively in the affairs of the Free Peoples when the cause is just, acting as informal agents of ManwÃ«'s will. Proud, aloof and not easily persuaded, they are nevertheless noble at heart and permanent allies to any who earn their gratitude.[file:88]

list.name: Stone Giants
list.description: Ancient, solitary beings of the high mountain vales; present since the First Age, the true indigenous inhabitants of the peaks. They avoid contact with all other races and are equally avoided; dangerous to encounter, especially during mountain thunderstorms when they hurl boulders in play. Not formally allied with or hostile to the Free Peoples, but deeply territorial.[file:88]

people_field_by_region.military: 100 Beornings. 60 Burgwards of Maethelburg. 900 Militia (3-4 weeks to raise, organized by clan). 135 Eagles (allies).
people_field_by_region.description: The region is sparsely populated by several distinct peoples: Northmen (Nenedain) in the Anduin Vales, Beornings on the eastern bank, Hillmen in the rough hills of Rhudaur, and Orcs in the mountain strongholds, alongside rarer presences of Elves, Dwarves, Giants and Eagles.

list.name: Northmen pastoralists
list.description: Semi-nomadic herders who graze their cattle along the riverbanks.

people_field_by_region.military: Local clan militia.
people_field_by_region.description: Inhabited by scattered Northmen herders and wandering Easterling scouts.

list.name: Wood-men of Southern Mirkwood
list.description: Clans of woodsmen based around Woodmen-town and smaller camps in the western eaves of the forest, living by woodcraft, hunting and small-scale farming and maintaining palisades and tactics suited to defending against Orcs and beasts.

list.name: Beijabar of Nan Anduin
list.description: Bear-folk and related Northmen dwelling in the Anduin vales south of the Old Forest Road, living in close symbiosis with animals and regarding certain sites along the river as holy, with reputations for hospitality but fierce defense of their lands.

list.name: Northmen of the Talath Harroch
list.description: Descendants of Vidugaviaâ€™s horse-lords in the plains and East Bight, formerly prosperous farmers and horse-breeders, reduced by the Plague to hard-edged defenders of isolated homesteads and fortified camps.

list.name: Eothraim and Gramuz tribes
list.description: Roving horse-tribes in the rolling hills and scattered woodlands, hunting great stags, herding horses and moving between seasonal camps with strong warrior traditions.

list.name: Gondorian outposts and settlers
list.description: Gondorians of Calenardhron and Dor RhÃ»nen who garrison forts like Thorontir and farm pockets such as Tharlorien and the Undeeps, guarding the fords and asserting Gondorâ€™s claimed dominion.

list.name: Asdriags and Sagath (Easterlings)
list.description: Eastern tribes with favored camps east of the plains, periodically raiding and trading, and deeply entangled in Gondorâ€™s campaigns and rivalries across Southern Rhovanion.

list.name: Elves of Southern Mirkwood
list.description: Silvan and Sindarin Elves in the northern reaches of the southern forest, heirs to Greenwood the Greatâ€™s earlier glory, maintaining hidden dwellings and circles in Angaladadh and along the forest roads.

list.name: Radagast the Brown
list.description: The Wizard dwelling at Rhosgobel on the western edge of Mirkwood, devoting his power and attention to birds and beasts rather than politics, and providing a rare point of light and refuge in the shadowed woods.

list.name: The Necromancer of Dol Guldur
list.description: Sauron in his guise as the Necromancer, occupying Dol Guldur since the 12th century T.A., radiating fear and corruption, breeding Orcs, trolls, spiders and fell beasts, and acting as the dark center of Southern Mirkwood.

list.name: Orcs and Tereg trolls of Dol Guldur
list.description: Sauronâ€™s garrison in Dol Guldur and the Emyn Guldur, using the hill as a fortress, prison, armory and staging point for raids into Mirkwood, Nan Anduin and the plains.

people_field_by_region.military: 650 Warriors, led by local chieftains.
people_field_by_region.description: Southern Rhovanion is a country under siege: Beijabar and Wood-men cling to the western eaves, horse-lords and Gramuz Northmen fight for survival on the Talath Harroch, Gondor projects power along the Anduin and into Dor RhÃ»nen, Easterling tribes press from the east, and above all Dol Guldur and its Necromancer darken the forest and shape the fate of all inhabitants after the Great Plague.

people_field_by_region.description: Home to Beornings (Beijabar), Woodmen, and pastoral Northmen.

list.name: Elves of Mithlond
list.description: High Elves who keep watch over the Elostirion tower and the road to the west.

people_field_by_region.symbol: CÃ­rdan's Device: A White Pelannun on a Blue Field
people_field_by_region.military: Elven archer squads.
people_field_by_region.description: Inhabited by high elves of Lindon and occasional Hobbit travelers.

list.name: Hillmen of Rhudaur (Rhudaurrim / MÃ´r Ithvaen clans)
list.description: The Hillmen are indigenous, short, dark, hardy folk inhabiting the rugged fells and highlands of Rhudaur. They exploit the jagged hills, boggy vales and wooded slopes for hunting, herding and raiding, and their tales emphasize the 'unknown dangers' of Trollshaws and Ettenmoors. Their raiding over the Oiolad ('Endless Plain') into more favored regions is a recurring theme in Arnorâ€™s frontier conflicts.[file:132]

list.name: Rhudaurrim (mixed DÃºnedain and Hillmen)
list.description: The term Rhudaurrim encompasses the kingdomâ€™s mixed population: a small, historically ruling DÃºnedain element and a larger Hillman base. Geographical descriptions note Rhudaurrim and Hillmen raiding across the Oiolad into neighboring lands, and their presence in central areas like the Trollshaws and Angle, where they share terrain with Trolls, wolves and losrandir.[file:132]

list.name: Trollshaws settlers and garrisons
list.description: Aside from Cameth Brin, few hills in the Trollshaws rise more than 800 feet, and permanent settlements are rare. The main human presence consists of fortified sites (e.g., Cameth Brin) and scattered farmsteads in safer pockets, always under threat from Hill Trolls, Forest Trolls and wolves.[file:132]

people_field_by_region.military: 4,500 Warriors and Eastern Mercenaries, usually grouped according to tribe.
people_field_by_region.population: 50,000: 35,000 Hillmen in thirteen tribes and 15,000 Dunlendings in eleven tribes.
people_field_by_region.description: Rhudaurâ€™s population is sparse and concentrated in hill-country and strategic sites such as Cameth Brin and the Angle. Politically, it is the easternmost and least stable of Arnorâ€™s successor realms, dominated by Hillmen and later influenced or controlled by Angmar. The Arnor bookâ€™s detailed cultural and political treatment is split across Arnor: The Land and Arnor: The People; here we focus on the geographic side, noting the Hillmen and Rhudaurrim as the characteristic inhabitants of this harsh landscape.

list.name: Men of Rhudaur
list.description: Men of the former kingdom of Rhudaur; many soldiers, especially land-hungry peasants, enlist in Angmarâ€™s armies and later settle as soldier-farmers in fortified villages.

list.name: Men of RÃ»n
list.description: Eastern (â€˜Rhunishâ€™) peoples from rigidly hierarchical, warlike cultures; peasant soldiers can rise in status through service in Angmar and retire as relatively prosperous veterans on frontier farms.

list.name: Estarav (Northrons of the Anduin Valleys)
list.description: Northmen of the Great River valleys; some young men, especially those with strong warrior values, join the Witch-kingâ€™s forces seeking wealth and opportunity.

list.name: Freeholders of fortified villages
list.description: Ex-soldiers of Angmar settled as freemen in fortified villages along the borders; they farm, patrol daily, escort supply trains, and provide additional troops in wartime.

list.name: Slaves
list.description: Captives and subjugated people from various backgrounds who work the land to support the warrior elite, although the harsh climate forces some warriors to continue agricultural labor.

list.name: Orcs of Angmar
list.description: Bulk of the Witch-kingâ€™s infantry, garrisoning forts, border castles, and strongholds throughout the realm.

list.name: The Witch-king of Angmar
list.description: Supreme ruler of Angmar and lord of the NazgÃ»l; directs long-term political and military strategy based on attrition, terror, and manipulation of frontier peoples.

people_field_by_region.description: Angmarâ€™s population mixes free and enslaved men with strong contingents of professional soldiers and ex-soldiers from warrior cultures such as Rhudaur, RÃ»n, and the Estarav of the Anduin, supported by orcs, trolls, and other creatures in the Witch-kingâ€™s service.

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Descendants of the Firebeards and Broadbeams who maintained Belegost and Nogrod. They are skilled miners, smiths, and weapon-crafters, mining abundant copper, iron, and tin.

list.name: Mountain Giants
list.description: Extremely long-lived, reclusive beings of immense size who dwell in the high peaks and avoid contact with other races.

people_field_by_region.symbol: A Hammer surmounted by Three Tongues of Flame
people_field_by_region.military: 490 Warriors. 2,100 Warrior Levy.
people_field_by_region.description: The Ered Luin is primarily inhabited by the Dwarves of Nogrod and Belegost's descendants, alongside scattered Elven wanderers and mountain Giants.

list.name: Hillmen shepherds
list.description: Hardy nomadic herders who graze their sheep in the southern valleys.

people_field_by_region.military: None.
people_field_by_region.description: Sparsely populated by wandering hillmen and isolated shepherds.

list.name: Falathrim (Sea-Elves)
list.description: Descendants of the ancient mariners of the Falas, the Falathrim are the primary coastal Elves of Lindon, inhabiting Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond and maintaining shipbuilding and ocean-voyage traditions. They form the backbone of Lindonâ€™s maritime culture and operate the Grey Havens as the main port to Valinor.[file:99]

list.name: Godhellim (Noldor of Lindon)
list.description: Noldorin Elves (Godhellim) under Gil-galad, primarily residing in Forlindon; they provide high leadership, craft and lore. Population tables show their proportion shrinking steadily from the Second to the late Third Age as many depart for Aman, while Sindar and Nandor proportions rise.[file:99]

list.name: Iathrim and Laegrim (Sindar and Laiquendi)
list.description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindonâ€™s woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

list.name: Lindellim and other Nandor/Mithrim houses
list.description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and RhÃ»nlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of LhÃ»n; Thorinâ€™s Halls (Durinâ€™s Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

list.name: Hobbits and Men along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ route
list.description: Hobbits of the Shire and Men of Cardolan do not form part of Lindonâ€™s resident population, but the Travellerâ€™s Guide describes their lands along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ 600-mile route from Imladris to Mithlond via Eregion, the South Downs, Barrow-downs, Old Forest and Brandywine. Tooks in Harlindon and Shire traditions about departures from the Grey Havens illustrate ongoing contacts.[file:99]

people_field_by_region.symbol: CÃ­rdan's Device: A White Pelannun on a Blue Field
people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
people_field_by_region.description: Lindonâ€™s population is overwhelmingly Elvish, composed of multiple lineages (Noldor, Sindar, Laiquendi, Nandor, Falathrim) under Gil-galad and later CÃ­rdan, with a significant Dwarven presence in the Blue Mountains and peripheral contact with Men and Hobbits via routes and harbors. list.name: Angmarean Patrols
list.description: Orc scouts and Mannish soldiers operating from border towers like Cargash.

list.name: Rangers of the North
list.description: DÃºnedain rangers monitoring the movements of the Witch-king's armies.

people_field_by_region.military: Sauron's orc warbands, warg-riders, Ranger scouts.
people_field_by_region.description: Entirely devoid of permanent settlements; traversed only by soldiers, orc warbands, and Dunlending mercenaries.

list.name: Arthadan frontier folk and militias
list.description: Frontier shepherds, small steads and watch-forts on the northwestern marches of Arthedain use the fringes of Talath Muil for grazing and as a staging ground, but the bleak climate and exposure prevent large-scale settlement. During the wars with Angmar, local militias are pinned on frontiers and armies cross these uplands, as described in the accounts of battles around Fornost and the Emyn Uial.[file:179][file:132]

list.name: Lossoth and northern neighbours (peripheral)
list.description: The description notes that even the hardy Lossoth shun this windswept plain; they are present more as a climatic benchmark than as inhabitants, emphasizing Talath Muilâ€™s severity even by northern standards.[file:179]

list.name: Orcs, Trolls and bandits (non-human threats)
list.description: While not 'people' in a civic sense, Orcs, Trolls and bandits regularly use the rocky Evendim fringe and Talath Muil as hiding and transit zones, preying on caravans, scouts and retreating armies. The module explicitly calls out their presence in this terrain.[file:179]

people_field_by_region.description: Talath Muil sits at the far edge of Arthedainâ€™s settled world. It has no dense population; its human presence consists of frontier shepherds, border watchposts and moving armies during the wars against Angmar. Even hardy Lossoth avoid this 'drear plain.'

list.name: Faithful of Arnor (religious communities)
list.description: For a time the Rammas, like the lower but more abrupt down-lands to the south, serves as a religious retreat for the Faithful of Arnor. Monastic houses, hermitages and small religious enclaves occupy hilltops and defiles, combining contemplative life with subsistence gardening and herding in the sheltered pockets of the ridge.

list.name: Arthadan garrisons and fort line
list.description: When the tide of war turns against Arthedain in T.A. 1325, a line of fortifications is constructed along the Rammas, anchored on its eastern flank by the great keep of Dol Gormaen. After the fall of that citadel in T.A. 1408, the ridge becomes an exposed frontier, fiercely contested by Arthadan and Angmarean forces, with blockhouses, watchposts and fieldworks strung along the hills.

list.name: Angmarean forces and raiders
list.description: Following Dol Gormaenâ€™s fall, Angmar gains direct access to the ridge. Orcs, human contingents and other servants of the Witch-king use the Rammas as a corridor for raids southwards. Anyone wandering here risks stumbling into a battle or being seized as a suspected spy by either side.

list.name: LumÃ«nyihmÃ«nyt
list.description: The LumÃ«nyihmÃ«nyt roam the northernmost stretches of the Rammas Forod from early spring through mid-autumn. Their camps, perched on rocky slopes, are crude hunting sites intended to last days or at most weeks. Reserved and wary, they usually break camp and move rather than deal with rough strangers, but if approached carefully they can provide detailed information about local geography, hazards and notable features.

people_field_by_region.description: In the late Third Age before the War of the Ring, the Rammas Forod functions as both a fortified frontier between Arthedain and Angmar and a zone of religious retreat. Permanent population is sparse, concentrated in strongpoints, tiny farmsteads in sheltered defiles and seasonal hunter camps along the northern edge.

list.name: Commons of Arthedain (Men of the Twilight Hills)
list.description: The 'Commons of Arthedain, and particularly the Men of the Twilight Hills,' are described as hard-working hillfolk who wrest grain, apples, mutton and herbs from steep slopes and thin soils. Though famed for 'bland frugality of speech and coin,' they are said to be 'happier than most' thanks to the unusual law and peace of Arthedain: 'a man can still walk the country from the River to the Downs with naught in his hand but a stick to ward off the stray dogs.'[file:179]

list.name: DÃºnedain lords of the Twilight Hills
list.description: DÃºnedain who rule the Emyn Uial are 'stern and aloof, but also just and fair-spoken to all.' They maintain roads, terraces, groves and law, and 'would have words with strangers who caused a fuss,' yet offer hospitality and advice to travellers. Their presence ensures comparatively safe travel and structural investment (roads, terraces) across the hills.[file:179]

list.name: Tinkers, peddlers and travellers
list.description: Tinkers and peddlers move from steading to steading along hardpacked and paved roads, doing odd jobs, selling goods and carrying news and gossip. They are described as friendly and talkative, equal in information value to merchants and often more approachable, shaping the social fabric of the hills.[file:179]

people_field_by_region.description: The Evendim Hills (Emyn Uial) are home primarily to the Commons of Arthedainâ€”the hillfolk of the Twilight Hillsâ€”with stern but just DÃºnedain lords. Their culture is shaped by hard labour, cool damp climate and relative peace under Arthadan rule. Later, wars and the Banes of Angmar depopulate the hills, but in your period they remain a living, if harsh, homeland.

list.name: Rangers of the North
list.description: Heirs of Arthedain who watch the ruins to prevent evil forces from occupying them.

people_field_by_region.military: Occasional Ranger patrols.
people_field_by_region.description: Deserted by civilized folk; only rangers and occasional tomb-robbers pass through.

list.name: Tom Bombadil
list.description: The oldest being, who sings songs to calm the angry trees of the forest.

people_field_by_region.military: The forest itself is its own defense.
people_field_by_region.description: Uninhabited by mortals; Hobbits fear to enter, and only Tom Bombadil dwells on its borders.

list.name: DÃºnedain of Arthedain
list.description: High Men descended from the Faithful of NÃºmenor, forming a relatively uniform but small population with long lifespans, great physical and mental strength, and a contemplative, Elven-influenced culture; they rule the kingdom from Fornost and maintain the line of kings.[file:25]

list.name: Lossoth of Forochel
list.description: The Snowmen of the far north, a poor, primitive, nomadic folk who roam the ice-plains north of Eriador and Angmar, hunting big game and living off the cold sea in a harsh environment.[file:25]

list.name: Bree-landers
list.description: Mixed folk of Bree and its environs, including men of various stocks and hobbits, living in and around a trenched, hedged village at the junction of the Greenway and Great East Road, once a vibrant trade-town but fading by T.A. 1640.[file:25]

list.name: Rivermen of the northern waters
list.description: River-folk who ply the Baranduin and other northern rivers, claiming the waters as their own and serving trade and communication between Arthedain, Lindon and other regions.[file:25]

list.name: Hobbits of the Shire
list.description: Small, hearth-loving folk who settled the Shire in T.A. 1601 under grant from King Argeleb II; they farm the rich southeastern lands of Arthedain and maintain a semi-autonomous, peaceful society that survives even the downfall of the kingdom.[file:25]

list.name: Elves of Lindon (nearby)
list.description: Though outside Arthedain proper, the Elves of Lindon and the Grey Havens are close neighbors across the LhÃ»n, providing crucial aid against Angmar and cultural and political support to the North-kingdom.[file:25]

people_field_by_region.military: Royal Forces: 540 Men at Arms. 370 Cavalry. 1,000 Rangers. Noble Forces: 2,150 Men at Arms. 620 Cavalry. 3,000 Levy.
people_field_by_region.population: 50,000 DÃºnedain (not including the Shire and Bree-land).
people_field_by_region.description: Arthedainâ€™s population is dominated by the high Men of the DÃºnedain, with important minorities including the hobbits of the Shire, the Lossoth of Forochel, rivermen on the northern waters, Bree-landers at the crossroads, and scattered Northmen and Dunlendish groups on the borders.

list.name: DÃºnedain of Cardolan
list.description: High Men descended from NÃºmenor who form the royal house, barons and professional soldiers of the Kingdom; they reach a peak of prosperity under King Tarandil but are later shattered by disasters like Cameth Brin and the Witchâ€‘kingâ€™s wars, with the last Prince Argil assassinated and survivors drifting to Arthedain and Gondor.[file:31]

list.name: Eriadoran Northmen and mercenaries
list.description: Tall blond horsemen from Rhovanion and other Northman stocks who settle as mercenaries and burhâ€‘folk, especially in Feotar and the eastern marches, later revolting against cruel lords and sometimes forming selfâ€‘governing communities.[file:31]

list.name: Dunlendings and Dunnish migrants
list.description: Descendants of the Daen Lintis who migrate into Cardolan from Enedhwaith and Dunland, forming hillâ€‘loving herders, Saralainn clans and later the Kingdom of Saralainn south of the GwathlÃ³ after Gordaigh Trollbaneâ€™s rise.[file:31]

list.name: Beffraen of Rast Vorn
list.description: A relatively primitive folk akin to DrÃºedain, inhabiting the dark woods of the Rast Vorn peninsula; they are secretive, use the treacherous forest and coasts to remain largely outside DÃºnadan control and maintain their own customs.[file:31]

list.name: Urban folk and guilds of Tharbad
list.description: Merchants, craftsmen, guildsmen, smugglers and refugees crowd the fenâ€‘ringed city of Tharbad, whose power shifts from Cardolanâ€™s kings to Gondorâ€™s Canotar and then to powerful guilds as war, flood and plague erode royal authority.[file:31]

list.name: Saralainn clans and southern tribes
list.description: Rowdy, semiâ€‘independent clans of Saralainn and the GwathlÃ³ mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]

people_field_by_region.military: Each Prince and Lord commands about 100 warriors in a personal retinue and can raise 200-800 levy troops in 2-3 weeks. Most often, however, the Lords rely upon mercenary bands and the Dunnish tribes in the region for military muscle. Gondor and Arthedain also maintain garrisons in Cardolan to ensure the safety of the trade routes.
people_field_by_region.population: 48,000 Cardolani (not including populations of Saralainn or the Cantons).
people_field_by_region.description: Cardolanâ€™s population mixes DÃºnedain princes and warriors, Eriadoran Northmen, Dunlendings, Beffraen and later southern refugees; after the Kingdomâ€™s fall the land fragments into rival principalities, mercenary companies, peasant rebels, Gondorian garrisons and eventually banditâ€‘ridden ruins under the shadow of Angmar.

list.name: Othraim (Horseâ€‘clans of southern Rhovanion)
list.description: Six tribes of Northman horsemen occupying the southern Rhovanion plain until T.A. 1856â€“99; semiâ€‘nomadic herders who called themselves the Othraim, ancestral culture of both Othod and Rohirrim.

list.name: Othod (Horseâ€‘folk of the Anduin vales)
list.description: Descendants of Northman refugees who migrated across the Anduin after the Wainrider wars and settled first near the Gladden Fields and later in the upper Anduin vales; united horseâ€‘folk who became known as the Othod.

list.description: Short, stocky Wild Men inhabiting primeval woodlands like Taur Andrast and other remnants; unexcelled woodcrafters who carve PÃºkelâ€‘men and sometimes interact with Rohan in times of war.

people_field_by_region.description: The Horseâ€‘lords are a wandering people whose history passes through three main stagesâ€”othraim in southern Rhovanion, othod in the vales of the Anduin, and Rohirrim in Rohanâ€”remaining semiâ€‘nomadic and horseâ€‘centered but evolving from scattered clans to a unified kingdom at the heart of western trade and war.

people_field_by_region.description: The Horseâ€‘lords are a wandering people whose history passes through three main stagesâ€”othraim in southern Rhovanion, othod in the vales of the Anduin, and Rohirrim in Rohanâ€”remaining semiâ€‘nomadic and horseâ€‘centered but evolving from scattered clans to a unified kingdom at the heart of western trade and war.

list.name: Falathrim (Sea-Elves)
list.description: Descendants of the ancient mariners of the Falas, the Falathrim are the primary coastal Elves of Lindon, inhabiting Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond and maintaining shipbuilding and ocean-voyage traditions. They form the backbone of Lindonâ€™s maritime culture and operate the Grey Havens as the main port to Valinor.[file:99]

list.name: Godhellim (Noldor of Lindon)
list.description: Noldorin Elves (Godhellim) under Gil-galad, primarily residing in Forlindon; they provide high leadership, craft and lore. Population tables show their proportion shrinking steadily from the Second to the late Third Age as many depart for Aman, while Sindar and Nandor proportions rise.[file:99]

list.name: Iathrim and Laegrim (Sindar and Laiquendi)
list.description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindonâ€™s woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

list.name: Lindellim and other Nandor/Mithrim houses
list.description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and RhÃ»nlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of LhÃ»n; Thorinâ€™s Halls (Durinâ€™s Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

list.name: Hobbits and Men along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ route
list.description: Hobbits of the Shire and Men of Cardolan do not form part of Lindonâ€™s resident population, but the Travellerâ€™s Guide describes their lands along the Wandering Companiesâ€™ 600-mile route from Imladris to Mithlond via Eregion, the South Downs, Barrow-downs, Old Forest and Brandywine. Tooks in Harlindon and Shire traditions about departures from the Grey Havens illustrate ongoing contacts.[file:99]

people_field_by_region.symbol: CÃ­rdan's Device: A White Pelannun on a Blue Field
people_field_by_region.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
people_field_by_region.description: Lindonâ€™s population is overwhelmingly Elvish, composed of multiple lineages (Noldor, Sindar, Laiquendi, Nandor, Falathrim) under Gil-galad and later CÃ­rdan, with a significant Dwarven presence in the Blue Mountains and peripheral contact with Men and Hobbits via routes and harbors. The Travellerâ€™s Guide provides detailed population tables showing long-term decline through the Third Age.

list.name: Orcs of the Mountains
list.description: Principal goblin tribes of the northern Misty Mountains, united under the lord of Gundabad as Ashdurbuk, the â€˜One Allâ€‘Rulerâ€™, providing levies and tribute to Angmar and fielding enormous war hosts in the Trollshaws, Eriador, and Rhovanion.

list.name: Goblinâ€‘king of Gundabad
list.description: Title for the tyrannical overlord who rules Mount Gundabad and claims suzerainty over all Orc tribes of the mountains; his court in the spires is rife with intrigue, assassination, and revolt.

list.name: Priests of Darkness
list.description: Orcish priests who preserve records of goblin deeds over two Ages and guard secret lore and rituals in hidden chambers, including tales of the coming from Morgothâ€™s realm.

list.name: Slaves of Gundabad
list.description: Captives from the Free Peoples, brought to labor in mines, Pits, and forges without hope of release, sustained only by fading memories of freedom.

people_field_by_region.description: Mount Gundabad is overwhelmingly an Orc realm, ruled by a Goblinâ€‘king recognized as overlord of the Orcs of the Mountains under the distant suzerainty of the Witchâ€‘king of Angmar; it also holds numerous slaves from surrounding lands.

list.name: Lumimiehet (Lossoth tundra clans)
list.description: The archetypal Snowmen of southern lore, the Lumimiehet are nomadic YstvÃ¤t Talven who range the Wash, Herd and Lakeland Tundras, following herds and living in small family bands bound into animal-totem clans.[file:39]

list.name: JÃ¤miÃ«het
list.description: A land-focused YstvÃ¤t Talven sub-culture relying heavily on herd management and inland hunting, occupying central Talath Uichel and interacting with both Lossoth and Angmarâ€™s frontier forces.[file:39]

list.name: YstvÃ¤t Talven hunterâ€“herders (general)
list.description: Across Talath Uichel, YstvÃ¤t Talven communities time their movements to cycles of herd migration, fur hunting and fishing, organizing life around kinship, totems and seasonal camps.[file:39]

list.name: Snow-elves of Helloth
list.description: The Lossidil Snow-elves maintain Helloth, an enchanted ice city on the Landless Land, and patrol the polar wastes and Talath Uichelâ€™s far north, guarding Helecthilâ€™s radiance and intervening occasionally in mortal affairs.[file:39]

list.name: Umli
list.description: Bearded folk of mixed Mannishâ€“Dwarvish appearance who have migrated from eastern lands into parts of Talath Uichel, where they adapt to tundra life and trade with YstvÃ¤t Talven and Elves.[file:39]

list.name: Berninga frontier folk
list.description: Bear-cult Men associated with the Beornings on the Eriadorian frontier, occupying the Rammas Forod and Northern Marches, grazing sheep, trapping and acting as transition communities between forest and tundra.[file:39]

list.name: Angmarim and the Sled-horde
list.description: The Witch-kingâ€™s forces in Talath Uichel include Urdic Sled-horde warriors, Trolls, Wargs and Orcs, raiding Lossoth and guarding Angmarâ€™s northern approaches while remaining limited in number by the harsh land.[file:39]

people_field_by_region.description: Talath Uichel and the Landless Land are the main domains of the YstvÃ¤t Talven tundra folk, Snow-elves in their icy city, Angmarâ€™s sled-hordes and scattered frontier groups from Eriador; very few others can endure the conditions.[file:39]

list.name: Nenedain (Northmen of the Anduin Vales)
list.description: Tall, strong, fair, hardy men descended from the same ancestral stock as the Edain; they form the majority of the settled population of the Anduin Vales, living as farmers, pastoralists and merchants. Culturally influenced by Gondor through trade, they have developed minor urban centers like Maethelburg along the Anduin but remain mostly a scattered rural folk. Decimated by the Great Plague (T.A. 1635â€“36), which killed about half their number. On good terms with DÃºnedain and tolerable terms with Hillmen; deeply hostile to Orcs.[file:88]

list.disclaimer: ONLY ADD region id in entity Beornings

list.name: Hillmen of Rhudaur (NeDreubhan)
list.description: Short, dark, stocky men of the rugged hills of Rhudaur, distantly related to Dunlendings; superb mountaineers but deeply suspicious of outsiders, prone to treachery and ambush. Worship vengeful petty deities with blood sacrifice. Traditional enemies of both DÃºnedain and Northmen; greatly fear Elves. Some served in the hosts of the Witch-king of Angmar alongside Orcs, an uneasy alliance. Survive as hunters, gatherers and occasional brigands in the broken terrain.[file:88]

list.name: Orcs of Goblin-gate (Uruk-tarkhnarb)
list.description: The Orcs of the High Pass, led by the Great Goblin (Durba-hai, 'People-ruler'); an autonomous Orcish tribe paying nominal tribute to Angmar and Mount Gundabad. Composed of common Orcs (3â€“5 ft) and an elite of Uruk-hai (5â€“6 ft), who act as leaders and bodyguards. Expert miners, smiths and warriors; operate primarily at night (common Orcs are weakened by daylight; Uruk-hai are unaffected). The tribe holds the mountain passages by force and extorts or destroys all who cross without safe-conduct.[file:88]

list.name: Great Eagles of Eagles' Eyrie
list.description: The Great Eagles under their Lord hold a distinct political power in the regionâ€”not mere animals but intelligent beings of near-divine descent, capable of speech in Westron. They intervene selectively in the affairs of the Free Peoples when the cause is just, acting as informal agents of ManwÃ«'s will. Proud, aloof and not easily persuaded, they are nevertheless noble at heart and permanent allies to any who earn their gratitude.[file:88]

list.name: Stone Giants
list.description: Ancient, solitary beings of the high mountain vales; present since the First Age, the true indigenous inhabitants of the peaks. They avoid contact with all other races and are equally avoided; dangerous to encounter, especially during mountain thunderstorms when they hurl boulders in play. Not formally allied with or hostile to the Free Peoples, but deeply territorial.[file:88]

people_field_by_region.military: 100 Beornings. 60 Burgwards of Maethelburg. 900 Militia (3-4 weeks to raise, organized by clan). 135 Eagles (allies).
people_field_by_region.description: The region is sparsely populated by several distinct peoples: Northmen (Nenedain) in the Anduin Vales, Beornings on the eastern bank, Hillmen in the rough hills of Rhudaur, and Orcs in the mountain strongholds, alongside rarer presences of Elves, Dwarves, Giants and Eagles.

list.name: Noldor of Eregion
list.description: Exiled High Elves who founded the realm after the War of the Jewels; they are master architects, jewel-smiths, and scholars, concentrated in Ost-in-Edhil and in estates on the rolling hills to the north.

list.name: Gwaith-i-MÃ­rdain
list.description: The Brotherhood of Jewel-smiths, headquartered at the Mirdainthron near Ost-in-Edhil. They are a tightly knit order of smiths and crafters, strictly organized with ranks such as Lord Smiths, Master Smiths, Hall Masters, and Smiths, and they ultimately create the Rings of Power.

list.name: Sindar of Eregion
list.description: Grey Elves who dwell in and around Ost-in-Edhil, some bearing memories and grudges from Doriathâ€™s sack by Dwarves of Nogrod, making them more distrustful of Dwarves even while living beside Khazad-dÃ»m.

list.name: Silvan Elves (rural shepherds)
list.description: Rustic Silvan Elves in the sparsely inhabited triangle between the Sirannon and the Glanduin, living as shepherds and smallholders far from the dense Noldorin urban culture.

list.name: Dwarves of Khazad-dÃ»m
list.description: Allied Dwarves who trade extensively with the Noldor of Eregion, exchanging ores and craft for Elven goods and knowledge; their friendship is largely driven by shared interest in craft and learning.

list.name: Galadriel and Celeborn
list.description: Queen and King of Eregion in its early years, residing in a modest but elegant city house and a country estate; Galadriel is the political and prophetic mind pushing for alliance against Morgothâ€™s lingering evil.

list.name: Celebrimbor
list.description: Master Smith, founder of the Gwaith-i-MÃ­rdain and de facto leader of the craft community; arguably the second-greatest Elven smith after FÃ«anor, and the maker of the Three Rings.

people_field_by_region.description: Eregion is a Noldorin-founded realm ruled initially by Galadriel and Celeborn, with Celebrimbor and the Gwaith-i-MÃ­rdain dominating its crafts and politics. The population includes Noldor, Sindar, and Silvan Elves, with close ties to the Dwarves of Khazad-dÃ»m and scattered rustic Silvan shepherds in the less-settled triangle between the Sirannon and the Glanduin.

list.name: Orcs of Khazad-dÃ»m
list.description: Sauron's servants who plunder the ancient dwarven vaults.

people_field_by_region.symbol: Durin's Folk: Seven Stars in a Triangular Formation
people_field_by_region.military: Mansion Watch and Weapon Host.
people_field_by_region.description: Currently occupied by the Orc-hordes of the Misty Mountains and a few isolated dwarven parties.

list.name: Dunlendings (Daen Lintis)
list.description: Stocky, mediumâ€‘height Common Men with sparse brown hair and ruddy complexions, organized into clans of extended family bands; isolationist, superstitious and proud, they survive largely by hunting, gathering and herding, with strong raiding traditions and loose, often violent politics.[file:32]

list.name: Daen Iontis faction
list.description: Militant Dunnish faction exemplified by clan Arailt and their allies, bitterly hating DÃºnedain, closely linked to the Temple of Justice, and pushing for war and a harsh, sacrificial religion that twists old Daen beliefs.[file:32]

list.name: Daen Coentis faction
list.description: Clans such as Treforn, Roth and Ewen who consciously revive the â€˜Skilled Peopleâ€™ heritage of the White Mountains, value craft and old gods, and have invited DrÃºedain back to Caerdh Wood to rebuild templeâ€‘observatories and shared religious life.[file:32]

list.name: DÃºnedain landowners
list.description: Scattered DÃºnadan manors and estates, often old NÃºmenÃ³rean outposts like Palomirâ€™s estate or the Healersâ€™ Hall at Cillien, occupied by families who farm and herd with fortified houses and uneasy relations with surrounding clans.[file:32]

list.name: Stoors of New Maresh
list.description: A small Stoor Hobbit community living in burrows on hills rising from a marsh near the Dunstrem, traveling by poleâ€‘boats, trading herbs and fish, and protected by the treacherous wetlands and their own wariness of outsiders.[file:32]

list.name: DrÃºedain of Caerdh Wood
list.description: Three villages of DrÃºedain invited by Daen Coentis clans into Caerdh Wood, who build stone villages and act as priests and spiritual guides, reâ€‘establishing ancient Daenâ€‘DrÃºedain religious partnerships.[file:32]

list.name: Traders and smugglers
list.description: Merchants based in Larach Duhnnan and other sites move goods between clans and to Tharbad, while a hidden Smugglersâ€™ Hold grows gort and tartella herbs for Corsairs of Umbar using Dunnish and DÃºnadan frontmen.[file:32]

people_field_by_region.military: Each Chief keeps from 100-400 Warriors as a permanent retinue. Each Clan can also raise 1,400-3,200 Warriors given 1-4 weeks. Tribes favor light armor and carry spears and melee weapons predominately.
people_field_by_region.description: Dunland is dominated by Dunlendingsâ€”Daen Lintis highlanders descended from the Daen Coentisâ€”living in extended family bands within clans, but also includes DÃºnedain estates, Stoors, DrÃºedain in Caerdh Wood, organized traders, bards, healers and a powerful Darkâ€‘influenced Temple hierarchy.

list.name: MerimetsÃ¤stÃ¤jÃ¤t (Sea-hunters)
list.description: YstvÃ¤t Talven who dwell on moving icebergs and along the Bay of Forochelâ€™s shores, hunting whales, seals and walruses; they are stocky, short, fair-haired and remarkably resistant to cold and immersion, but suffer high mortality from the hazards of the sea.[file:39]

list.name: Helechoth / Aerfaroth (Lossoth of the Cape and Bay)
list.description: The Lossoth of the cape and bayâ€”called Icemen and Sea-hunters in Sindarinâ€”build snow houses, run on ice with bone skates and use wheel-less sled-carts; they camp along Forochelâ€™s shores at the feet of the Blue Mountains and maintain a harsh, proud independence.[file:39]

list.name: Snow-elves of Helloth
list.description: Snow-elves travel between the Landless Land, Helloth and Forochelâ€™s hinterlands; some venture to the bay to trade, while others remain in the enchanted city of ice guarding Helecthilâ€™s radiance.[file:39]

list.name: Lodge of Awakening (Noldorin mystics)
list.description: An obscure order of Noldorin mystics maintains Evermist, a hidden valley on the Cape of Forochel that serves as a haven for the Free Peoples and base for resistance to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:39]

list.name: Southern whalers and traders
list.description: Whaling ships and camps from Cardolan and Gondor exploit the rich marine resources of Forochelâ€™s bay, trading with or competing against local sea-hunters.[file:39]

people_field_by_region.description: Forochel is the heartland of the YstvÃ¤t Talven sea-hunters and tundra folk, but also hosts Snow-elves, Noldorin mystics at Evermist and southern whaling expeditions, all under the distant shadow of Angmar.[file:39]

list.name: Sauron's Armies
list.description: Huge armies of Orcs, Olog-hai (trolls), and Easterling/Haradrim auxiliaries garrisoning the black towers.

people_field_by_region.military: Durthang: 200 Gondorian Soldiers. Cirith Ungol: 100 Gondorian Soldiers. Cirith Gorgor: 300 Gondorian Soldiers. Conquered Host: 11,000 Orc Warriors. 2,170 Uruk-hai. 750 Warg-riders. 100 Trolls.
people_field_by_region.description: Warlike populations of Orcs, Trolls, Half-Orcs, and dark Men loyal to Sauron.

list.name: Gondorian rangers of Ithilien
list.description: By the War of the Ring, Ithilienâ€™s main Gondorian inhabitants are the Rangers under Faramir, who use secret refuges such as Henneth AnnÃ»n and move stealthily through the woods, observing and ambushing enemy forces.[web:221][web:226] They are DÃºnedain of Gondor or men closely allied with them, deeply familiar with the landâ€™s paths, streams and cover.

list.name: Displaced Gondorian folk (earlier Third Age context)
list.description: Earlier in the Third Age, Ithilien was one of Gondorâ€™s core provinces, governed from Minas Ithil and described as very fertile and well-settled.[web:224][web:225] Over time, growing threats from Mordor and raids forced most of its inhabitants back over the Anduin; remnants of villages, gardens and roads remain in North Ithilien as ruins and overgrown clearings.

list.name: Haradrim and other enemy forces in transit
list.description: During the War of the Ring, Haradrim and other allies of Sauron cross North Ithilien on their way to the Black Gate and the assaults on Gondor; Tolkien shows Frodo and Sam witnessing a Harad column and a mÃ»mak in this region.[web:225][web:226] These forces establish temporary camps, use the old roads and further disturb the land.

people_field_by_region.description: In Forithilienâ€™s late Third Age state, permanent civilian settlement has largely withdrawn across the Anduin, but its land still bears the imprint of Gondorâ€™s earlier occupation and its present use as a hidden war-front. The primary human presence is Faramirâ€™s rangers, operating from secret refuges among the woods and cliffs, with enemy movements from Mordor crossing the region along roads and tracks.

list.name: Slaves of Nurn
list.description: Captive Men from the East and South forced to work the vast agricultural fields.

people_field_by_region.description: Populated by tens of thousands of slaves and their Orc taskmasters.

list.name: Wild Men of DrÃºwaith Iaur
list.description: Native DrÃºedain (Woses) who live primitive lives in the most remote, inaccessible mountain valleys and deep woods, completely avoiding contact with the outside world.

list.name: Dunir (Dunmen)
list.description: Scattered, pastoral tribes of Dunlending descent who herd livestock in the foothills and maintain small, family-level family groups.

people_field_by_region.description: Entirely depopulated of Gondorian settlers; inhabited solely by native DrÃºedain and rustic Dunlending clans.

list.name: DÃºnedain of the Faithful
list.description: The NÃºmenÃ³rean descendants of the Faithful who settled Lebennin, Belfalas and the river valleys, governing the realm from Pelargir, Dol Amroth and other citadels; tall, long-lived and sea-faring, they form the nobility and officer class.[file:73]

list.name: Danan Lin / Daen folk (lowlands)
list.description: The indigenous pre-NÃºmenÃ³rean people of the coastlands and plains, shorter and stockier than the DÃºnedain, who adopted Westron and much of NÃºmenÃ³rean culture while retaining their oral traditions and ballads; they form the majority of the common population of Lebennin and Anfalas.[file:73]

list.name: Orodbedhrim / Daen Coentis (highlanders)
list.description: Mountain clans of the White Mountains living above the timberline in primitive groupings; semi-nomadic goat shepherds and fisherfolk on the coasts (Dunir of Andrast), they have largely avoided cultural contact with Gondor and still honor ancient Daen traditions.[file:73]

list.name: Sakalai (Ethir swamp peoples)
list.description: Small, dark-haired, independent tribes in the Loeg Balimur and remote Ethir Anduin, believed to be of mixed Daen and Southron stock; they live as swamp fishermen largely outside Gondorian authority.[file:73]

list.name: Grey-elves of Edhellond
list.description: A remnant Sindarin community dwelling at the fabled haven of white swan-ships near the mouth of the Morthond, closely allied with the Princes of Belfalas; guardians of the Eryn Laegol and builders of the most beautiful ships in Southern Gondor.[file:73]

people_field_by_region.description: Southern Gondor is the most populous and civilized part of Gondor, dominated by DÃºnedain nobility and the Daen-DÃºnedain mixed population of the river plains, with Daen highlanders (Orodbedhrim) in the upland vales, swamp peoples in the Ethir Anduin, and Grey-elves at Edhellond.

list.name: DÃºnedain of the Faithful
list.description: The NÃºmenÃ³rean descendants of the Faithful who settled Lebennin, Belfalas and the river valleys, governing the realm from Pelargir, Dol Amroth and other citadels; tall, long-lived and sea-faring, they form the nobility and officer class.[file:73]
